Weekly meal planner
Flag when running low — tap 🔴
🩺What your plants are trying to tell you. Tap any symptom to read more.
Leaves
💛 Yellow leaves
▼
Overwatering (most common)
  • Soil is soggy or smells musty
  • Leaves yellow from the bottom up
  • Fix: let soil dry fully, check drainage
Underwatering
  • Soil bone dry, leaves crispy at tips too
  • Whole plant looks droopy
  • Fix: deep water, then adjust schedule
Too little light
  • New growth is pale or leggy
  • Lower leaves yellow and drop
  • Fix: move closer to a window
Natural aging
  • Only 1–2 bottom leaves at a time
  • Rest of plant looks healthy
  • Normal — just remove the leaf
Leaves
🟫 Brown tips or edges
▼
Low humidity
  • Tips turn brown and crispy
  • Common in Elk Grove summers
  • Fix: mist, pebble tray, or move away from AC vents
Underwatering
  • Tips brown first, then whole edges
  • Soil very dry, leaves may curl
  • Fix: water more consistently
Fertilizer burn
  • Brown crispy edges on many leaves at once
  • Recently fertilized?
  • Fix: flush soil with plain water
Direct sun scorch
  • Pale or bleached brown patches
  • Usually on the side facing the window
  • Fix: move back or filter the light
Whole plant
😔 Drooping or wilting
▼
Thirsty (dry soil)
  • Stick your finger 2 inches in — dry?
  • Leaves limp but not yellowing
  • Fix: water thoroughly right now
Overwatered (root rot)
  • Soil wet but plant still wilting
  • Leaves may be yellowing too
  • Fix: unpot, check roots — brown/mushy = rot. Trim and repot in fresh dry soil
Too much heat
  • Wilts midday, perks back up at night
  • Common above 95°F in Elk Grove
  • Fix: move away from windows in summer
Root bound
  • Wilts even right after watering
  • Roots coming out the drainage holes
  • Fix: repot one size up
Leaves
🔴 Spots or patches
▼
Fungal infection
  • Brown or black spots with yellow halos
  • Spreads leaf to leaf
  • Fix: remove affected leaves, improve airflow, avoid wetting leaves when watering
Bacterial spot
  • Water-soaked dark spots, may ooze
  • Fix: remove leaves, keep dry, isolate plant
Spider mites
  • Tiny yellow/white speckling all over
  • Fine webbing under leaves
  • Fix: wipe leaves with damp cloth, neem oil spray
Cold water spots
  • White or pale spots appear after watering
  • No spread, no pattern
  • Fix: use room-temp water, water at soil not leaves
Pests
🐛 Bugs on the plant
▼
Fungus gnats
  • Tiny flies hovering around soil
  • Larvae damage roots
  • Fix: let soil dry more between waterings, sticky traps, hydrogen peroxide soil drench
Mealybugs
  • White fluffy cotton-like clusters
  • Usually in leaf joints and undersides
  • Fix: dab with rubbing alcohol on cotton swab, neem oil
Scale
  • Brown bumps on stems that don't wipe off
  • Sticky residue on leaves below
  • Fix: scrape off manually, neem oil, isolate plant
⚠️ Bird safety note
  • Never use chemical pesticides near Doc, Morgan, or Billy
  • Neem oil is safer but use with birds out of room
  • Isolate treated plants for 24–48 hrs
Growth
📏 Leggy or stretched out
▼
Not enough light
  • Long stems with lots of space between leaves
  • Plant leaning toward the window
  • Fix: move to brighter spot or rotate weekly
What to do
  • Prune leggy stems back to a node
  • This encourages bushier, fuller growth
  • Don't be afraid to cut — most plants bounce back fast
💡 General rule of thumb
Most plant problems in Elk Grove come down to three things: too much water, not enough light, or low humidity from the AC and summer heat. When in doubt — check the soil first. Soggy = back off. Bone dry = water. Everything else is details.
⚠️Even "safe" plants can cause issues if chewed heavily or soil/fertilizer is accessed. Supervise budgies near plants during out-of-cage time.
Spa chemistry — last logged
Spa log history
No entries yet. Log your first reading below.
Chemistry correction guide
Target: 3–5 ppm
🧪 Free Bromine
▼
📈 Too high (>5 ppm)
  • Don't add more bromine tabs/sticks
  • Remove the floater or pull tabs from feeder
  • Leave cover off — bromine dissipates with aeration
  • Wait 24 hrs and re-test before getting in
  • Skin/eye irritation is a sign it's too high
📉 Too low (<3 ppm)
  • Add more bromine tabs to floater/feeder
  • Or shock with sodium bromide + non-chlorine oxidizer (MPS) to "activate" the bromine reserve
  • Shock weekly with non-chlorine oxidizer (potassium monopersulfate)
  • Bromine works across a wider pH range than chlorine — but still check pH first
Target: 7.2–7.8
⚗️ pH Level
▼
📈 Too high (>7.8)
  • Add pH decreaser (dry acid / sodium bisulfate)
  • ~1 oz per 250 gal lowers pH ~0.2
  • Add near jets with pump running
  • Wait 30 min, retest before adjusting more
📉 Too low (<7.2)
  • Add pH increaser (sodium carbonate)
  • ~1 oz per 250 gal raises pH ~0.2
  • High pH = cloudy water, scale buildup
  • Low pH = skin/eye irritation, corrodes equipment
  • Bromine tolerates pH swings better than chlorine but still aim for 7.2–7.8
Target: 100–150 ppm
🔬 Total Alkalinity
▼
📈 Too high (>150 ppm)
  • Add pH decreaser in small doses over several days
  • Don't lower it too fast — affects pH
  • Cloudy water and scale are common signs
  • Partial water drain + refill also helps
📉 Too low (<100 ppm)
  • Add alkalinity increaser (sodium bicarbonate)
  • ~1.5 oz per 250 gal raises ~10 ppm
  • Low TA = pH swings wildly — fix this first
  • Add with jets running, wait 1 hr before retesting
Target: 150–250 ppm
💎 Calcium Hardness
▼
📈 Too high (>250 ppm)
  • Scale buildup on shell and jets
  • Partial drain and refill with fresh water
  • Use a calcium reducer product
  • Elk Grove tap water is moderately hard — check on refill
📉 Too low (<150 ppm)
  • Add calcium chloride (hardness increaser)
  • Low calcium is corrosive to the shell and heater
  • Add slowly — calcium raises temp of water when dissolved
  • Retest after 24 hrs
💡 Correction order matters
Always adjust in this order: Alkalinity first → pH second → Bromine last. Fixing TA stabilizes pH, and proper pH keeps bromine working efficiently. Skipping the order wastes chemicals and can make things worse.
⚠️Pool service comes Wednesdays — pool only, they don't handle the spa. Log spa readings separately 2–3x/week.

Parenting guide — Sterling, age 4
Real-world scripts and frameworks for hard moments. Tap any section to expand.
Meltdown scripts
What to actually say
▼
When it's starting
  • "I can see you're really upset right now."
  • "Your body is telling you it needs a break."
  • "Let's take some big breaths together."
Mid-meltdown (stay minimal)
  • "I'm right here." (then go quiet)
  • Sit nearby — don't lecture or reason yet
  • Fewer words = better. Wait it out.
After he calms down
  • "That was really hard. You made it through."
  • "Next time you feel that way, we can try…"
  • Address any behavior calmly — once, briefly
If it happens in public
  • Remove from the situation first, no explaining
  • "We're going to step outside for a minute."
  • Ignore bystanders — your kid needs you, not them
Remember
Fewer words, more presence. Your job mid-meltdown isn't to fix it — it's to outlast it calmly.
Natural consequences
How they actually work
▼
The rule of thumb
  • The consequence should be related to the behavior
  • It should happen soon — 4-year-olds need fast feedback
  • Say it once, then follow through quietly
Real examples
  • Throws food → meal ends, no snack yet
  • Won't put shoes on → misses the fun part of the outing
  • Rough with a toy → toy goes away for the rest of the day
  • Hits → play date ends immediately
What to avoid
  • Threats you won't follow through on
  • Consequences that punish you as much as him
  • Bringing it up again later — once it's done, it's done
What to say
  • "When you hit, we go home. Ready to try again tomorrow?"
  • "The toy will be here tomorrow when you're ready to be gentle."
  • No long explanations — state it, do it, move on
Remember
The consequence only works if you follow through every time. One exception teaches him to test every limit.
Bedtime routine
A consistent framework
▼
💡 Why the wind-down is non-negotiable
Four-year-olds often experience FOMO. If you jump straight from a high-energy game or cartoon to "Go to sleep," his brain will stay in high gear for another hour. The wind-down isn't optional — it's what makes the rest of the routine actually work.
~60 min before bed: wind down
  • Screens off — non-negotiable
  • Lower lights, lower voices, lower energy
  • Snack if needed (hunger = harder sleep)
The routine itself
  • Bath or wash up
  • Pajamas + brush teeth
  • 1–2 books (his choice = buy-in)
  • Quick chat: one good thing from today
  • Lights out, same time every night
Stall tactics — how to handle them
  • Build "one more" things IN: 2 books, 1 water, 1 hug
  • "That's on the list for tomorrow" closes most requests
  • Don't re-enter after lights out — consistency is everything
Pro tips
  • Visual routine chart on the wall = fewer arguments
  • Let him "check off" steps — 4-year-olds love control
  • Same routine on weekends matters as much as weekdays
Remember
Consistency is the whole game. A slightly imperfect routine done every night beats a perfect one done sometimes.
Managing your own frustration
When you're at your limit
▼
In the moment
  • Long exhale — longer out than in — activates calm
  • Drop your shoulders physically; it signals your brain
  • "I need a minute" is a complete sentence
  • Walk to another room if safe to do so
Reframes that help
  • "He's not giving me a hard time — he's having a hard time"
  • "This is developmental, not personal"
  • "My calm is the most powerful tool I have right now"
After a hard moment
  • It's okay to apologize to your kid — it models repair
  • Don't spiral in guilt — move forward
  • One rough interaction doesn't define your parenting
Big picture self-care
  • Your frustration tolerance is lower when you're depleted
  • Sleep, food, and a moment alone matter — protect them
  • Tag-team with a partner when you're at your limit
Remember
You can't regulate his emotions if yours are dysregulated. Your calm isn't just helpful — it's literally contagious to his nervous system.

Backup & restore
Save everything in this app (tasks, logs, snapshots, photos, notes) to a JSON file you can keep on your phone or a drive. Restore it later if data is ever lost or you want to roll back.

💡 Free up space
If your data meter is getting full, these are the biggest things you can trim:
📸 Photos
If photos are attached anywhere (vault, milestones), they're typically the biggest space user. Remove ones you've already saved elsewhere.
📝 Long notes & text fields
Trim old Sterling notes, shared notes, or vendor notes you no longer need.
📅 Old events & tasks
Delete completed one-off tasks and past calendar events from months ago.
💵 Utility history
You only need ~12 months of utility bills — remove older entries.
🛠️ Old milestones & wishlist
Clean out items that are no longer relevant.
